flashcards.front: State Bayes' Theorem as applied in Naive Bayes classification.
flashcards.back: P(C|X) = [P(X|C) × P(C)] / P(X), where C is class, X is feature vector. In practice, since P(X) is constant across classes, we predict: Ĉ = argmax P(C) × P(X|C).

flashcards.front: What is the 'naive' assumption in Naive Bayes, and why is it computationally beneficial?
flashcards.back: Features are conditionally independent given the class: P(X|C) = Πᵢ P(xᵢ|C). This decomposes high-dimensional joint probability into product of univariate distributions, making estimation tractable even with many features.

flashcards.front: What is Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation in Naive Bayes?
flashcards.back: MAP selects class maximizing posterior: Ĉ = argmax P(C|X) = argmax P(C) × P(X|C). We estimate P(C) as class frequency and P(X|C) from training data—dropping P(X) since it's constant across classes.

flashcards.front: Why is Laplace smoothing (additive smoothing) used? State its formula.
flashcards.back: Prevents zero probabilities when unseen feature-class combinations occur. Formula: P(xᵢ|C) = [Count(xᵢ, C) + α] / [Count(C) + α×|V|], where α≥1 (usually 1) and |V| is vocabulary size. With α=1, it's Laplace; α<1 is Lidstone.

flashcards.front: Gaussian Naive Bayes: How does it calculate likelihood for continuous features?
flashcards.back: Assumes each feature per class follows Normal distribution: P(xᵢ|C) = (1/√(2πσ²)) × exp[-(xᵢ-μ)²/(2σ²)], where μ and σ² are mean/variance of feature xᵢ in class C. Parameters estimated via MLE from training data.

flashcards.front: Compare Multinomial and Bernoulli Naive Bayes for text classification.
flashcards.back: Multinomial uses term frequencies—ideal for long documents; only considers feature presence (counts). Bernoulli uses binary features (word present/absent)—better for short texts, explicitly penalizes word absence: P(xᵢ|C) = P(i|C)xᵢ + (1-P(i|C))(1-xᵢ).

flashcards.front: Given training data: Class 'Yes' (docs: 3), Class 'No' (docs: 3), word 'offer' appears in 3 'Yes' and 1 'No' docs. Calculate P('offer'|Yes) with Laplace smoothing (α=1, |V|=100).
flashcards.back: P('offer'|Yes) = (3 + 1) / (3 + 100) = 4/103 ≈ 0.0388. Numerator: Count('offer',Yes)+α = 3+1=4. Denominator: Count(Yes)+α×|V| = 3+100=103. This avoids zero probability for unseen words.

flashcards.front: State two key strengths and two limitations of Naive Bayes as a classifier.
flashcards.back: Strengths: (1) Computationally efficient O(d×n) training/prediction; (2) Handles high-dimensional data well with limited samples. Limitations: (1) Independence assumption often violated in practice; (2) Zero-frequency problem requires smoothing; (3) Poor probability estimates (calibration issues).
